author: Samira
body:
Thirty-Seven Weeks

Thirty-seven weeks. I was at the office.

I had been carrying a low unease for weeks. Too many busy days. I had fallen recently. I went to the ER the next day. Then a few times more, just to be sure. The midwife appointment was in two days. Things were starting to feel real.

The weekend before, I finally washed the few baby clothes we had bought. We picked the hospital bag. Inside it was one box of toasted mini breads. That was all.

At lunch, walking with colleagues, a gush. Did I just pee? I sat down to eat. Every laugh, every shift in my chair, more water. I had felt this before, smaller, here and there, and dismissed it. This time I couldn't.

I called my husband. I don't have things under control. Maybe come pick me up.

I googled in the bathroom. Even when waters break early, contractions usually start soon after. I told myself I'd wait until I was sure. I didn't want another ER trip. And I was embarrassed. What if I called and they told me I had just peed myself.

I stayed home the next day. Friends were visiting from out of town. We had wanted to show them the city before the baby came.

In the morning everyone left the house. Our friends to do a gallery tour. My husband to work. I stayed home. The plan was to meet back here for an early dinner, then drive up to Twin Peaks for the night view of the city.

I was standing at the door, still talking to our friends as they were heading out, when I felt another gush.

More of it through the day. Coming and going. Not enough to be sure. Enough to keep me circling the same question. Was this really my water? Back and forth with our doula on the phone. Maybe wait. Maybe not. By early evening the discharge was different. Sticky, faintly yellow. I called the clinic. After hours. They said go to the ER. Bring the bag, just in case.

The bag was empty.

I called my husband. He didn't answer. I rushed through a shower, threw random things into the bag, called a taxi. Our friends arrived as I was waiting on the curb. I just need to go to the hospital for a quick test. I'll be back. They wouldn't let me go alone. We cancelled the taxi. On the drive, I wasn't thinking about the baby. I was thinking about the work I hadn't finished. About my career. About what my colleagues would think if I disappeared.

Thursday evening. A few hours in the ER. PROM confirmed.

Can we go home and come back tomorrow?

I wouldn't be going home, they said, until the baby was born.

But my driver's license test is tomorrow. My midwife appointment.

We were completely unprepared. My husband went home for the things we still hadn't packed. I sat on the hospital bed with my laptop open, sending Slack messages, trying to finish reports, like I could outrun this by working through it.

They wanted induction now. Infection risk. I wanted time. I wanted my body to start on its own.

It got chaotic immediately. Each shift, a new face, a new opinion.

The first attending I met after I was admitted to the room was forceful. She wanted to skip steps. I resisted. I asked questions. I said I wanted to wait until the next stage of the induction process before pushing ahead. She made me feel I was risking the baby's life by asking. We don't have time. You don't understand the risk. That tone.

I remember the faces of every nurse and staff member in that room. The way they looked at each other when I asked. The way they looked at me. Like I was being unreasonable. Like I was being crazy. A first-time mother with a birth plan who hadn't read the room.

After her shift ended, a nurse said, quietly, that the approach had seemed unusual. I was too tired to hold onto it.

They started Pitocin. Through the night and the next day they raised the dose. I felt some contractions but barely. A strange pain that wasn't labor pain. They kept checking my cervix.

The whole time, I was tethered. The monitor straps across my belly. Wires running off the bed. The IV in the back of my hand, the line catching on everything. The thing in my hand hurt. It throbbed when I moved my wrist the wrong way.

I could move. It was just hard. I had to drag everything with me, the pole, the wires, the monitor, and every time something slipped or shifted, the machines beeped, and someone came in to fix it. A little annoyed. A little tight in the face. Like I was the one making trouble. Like I should sit still.

Outside it was raining like hell. Hours of it. Days of it. The kind of rain that doesn't let up, doesn't break, just keeps going while you watch it through a window you can't open.

By early Saturday morning they said they had hit max dose. Four centimeters. They paused and told me to rest.

That's when I started reading.

My cervix had not been favorable when they started. Their own notes said so. I asked them about it. They acknowledged a mistake might have been made. They said they could still make it work.

I held my husband and cried, not knowing exactly why.

Our doula was on the phone with us all night and came early in the morning. She tried everything. Exercises. Encouragement. Soft voice. I was somewhere far inside myself. Nothing reached me. I clung to the only thing I still recognized. The birth plan. Showed it to every new nurse. Walked through every line. Then closed it. Opened the laptop. Went back to work.

Between the checks and the rising doses, I did everything I could think of. I walked the hallways dragging the IV pole, my husband on one side, the doula on the other. He never left. He held my arm on the laps. Counted breaths on the ball. Learned the lunges and the hip openers with me. Our friends, the ones who drove me here, kept coming back. Food we could actually eat. Snacks for the long nights. Small things from home.

Everyone was trying so hard. Mostly they were trying to distract me. The doula put up little decorative lights around the room. Small soft glows, trying to make it feel less like a hospital. She sat with me and we drew. There was this thing I did, drawing random lines and curves on the page, then coloring in the spaces between, finding shapes inside the mess. I kept finding fishes. Page after page. Different fishes, swimming through the lines. I don't know why. My husband stayed close, talking, holding the room together with his voice. Our friends came and went, bringing food, sitting with us when we needed company. They kept telling me to rest. Rest. Try to sleep. You'll need your energy.

I couldn't rest. I kept thinking about what I should do, what position, what movement, what I hadn't tried yet, to help the baby be born. Lying still felt like giving up. Closing my eyes felt like giving up. I saw what everyone was doing. I loved them for it. I couldn't climb out of where I was sinking. Their kindness landed on me like rain on a closed window.

I bounced on the ball. Squats. Stairs when they let me. New position every time someone suggested one. I wanted, more than anything, to give her a way out that wasn't a scalpel. If movement could do it, I'd move. If patience could do it, I'd wait. I was trying to meet my body halfway. Hoping it would meet me back.

Family kept calling. Video chats with both sides, time zones away, faces leaning in toward their phones. Every time they called, they were waiting for us to say it. Is she here? Is the baby here? I could see them starting to worry. The pauses got longer. The questions got softer. How are you? Really, how are you? But I couldn't reach the worry myself. I noticed I was strangely calm. Like the part of me that should have been afraid was somewhere I couldn't get to.

Saturday morning they restarted. One dose of miso, then back to Pitocin.

Next exam: two centimeters.

Two. Not four.

Shift change. New doctor. We should seriously consider a C-section. Risk of hemorrhage was rising.

I hadn't slept in two nights. I could barely speak. I cried without sound. Nothing was going the way I had planned. Natural birth. Minimal intervention. As little hospital time as possible. I was somewhere I didn't recognize. In a body I couldn't steer.

Wait and try again, or surgery.

The staff were disagreeing with each other in front of me.

By that point I didn't trust any of them. The first attending who had pushed past my questions. The shifts that contradicted each other. The notes that admitted a mistake might have been made and then carried on like it didn't matter. Each new face had a new opinion and none of them were going to be in this room when the consequences came. Whatever happened next, it would happen to me and to her, and we would carry it. Not them.

And we had to decide. Now. With no sleep. With my body shaking. With a stranger I had met an hour ago telling me what was safest. With another stranger across the room shaking her head. Wait or surgery. The two doors. Pick one. The weight of it sat on my chest. If we waited and something went wrong with her, I would never forgive myself. If we did the surgery and I could have given her one more hour, I would never forgive myself either. There was no answer that didn't cost something. There was no one who could carry it for us.

I asked for one more exam.

One centimeter.

I looked at the doula. Is that even possible?

Until that moment I had been certain I could endure any pain to bring her safely into the world. I had not, in any serious way, considered that one of us might not come out of this. The last few days had taken everything I had, layer by layer, until almost nothing was left.

A little after they cut the Pitocin and the nurse finished the last exam, I started to shake. Uncontrollably. And then, finally, real contractions. Strong ones. As if my body had been waiting for the drugs to clear before it would speak.

It was too late.

My husband and I decided. Safest path for the baby. C-section.

I was very scared. Some part of me still believed if they would just wait a little longer, labor would come on its own. Once we agreed, everything moved at a speed I could not match.

OR. Thirsty. Cold. Shaking. Couldn't stop crying. They asked me to sit still for the epidural. I physically could not stop shaking. I kept thinking how much I had wanted her born without all of this. That if they would just wait.

Everything was moving without me.

I blacked out in pieces. Voices talking to my husband about the baby. She was born quickly. I was fighting not to fall asleep. Lost for a moment. Saw my sister in a dream. They put her on my chest for a few seconds, then took her away with my husband out of the room.

I remember the warmth of her skin against my cheek.

I woke up nauseous. The room was full of people. I thought my husband and the baby were just there in the corner. I could almost feel them, like they hadn't gone. Then I heard someone say the husband had left with the baby. I had nodded for him to go. I knew that. I had wanted her not to be alone. But hearing it out loud, I felt a small sad thing slip through me. They had left. The baby was born and they were both somewhere else and I was here. Like I wasn't important anymore. Like once she was out, I was the one who could be left behind.

(Later, when I heard my husband's side of the story, none of this was true at all. But this is what I felt, lying there.)

The anesthesia resident was talking to me, low voice, trying to keep me with him.

There had been a hemorrhage. They couldn't stop the bleeding. They were working on it.

My mind drifted in and out. I asked a nurse why it was taking so long. Complications. A bit longer.

Hours. Eventually they brought me to recovery. My husband was there with the baby. I was so cold I could barely nod. So thirsty I could only think of water. He was telling me how beautiful she was, and I turned to the nurse and asked if I could have something to drink.

I think I believed I might not make it. Looking at the two of them, I told myself if anything happened, at least she would be taken care of. That was the whole shape of my thinking.

A few sips of sweetened water. It tasted like heaven. I could feel the cells of my brain waking up, one by one.

The first hours, maybe the whole first day, I was outside myself. I couldn't move freely. The baby wasn't with me most of the time, even though I wanted her so badly.

When we first got to the room, the nurse put her on my chest for a few minutes. I was afraid I'd break her. So small. So fragile. I had imagined this moment for months and now I could barely hold the weight of it.

Then they left us to sleep. I had asked my husband to push the crib up against my bed. It still felt too far. I lay there watching her, watching him sleep, wanting to reach for her and unable to lift my hand that far.

So I held the crib instead.

That first day she was with me, but not really. She was in the crib. The nurses came in to check her. My husband changed her. They only brought her up to me when it was time to feed. She wasn't doing well. My husband kept telling the nurses she seemed cold, that she needed warmth. No one suggested the simplest thing. Skin to skin. They told me to rest.

I wish I had insisted. I wish I had kept her close.

At one point they said she was breathing too fast and took her away for tests. I was stuck in the room, unable to stand, calling and asking when they would bring her back. After the first day they took out the catheter, the drain. The IV stayed in my hand until the last day. My hand ached and bruised under it. The small mercy was that, by then, I could move enough to walk over to her when I wanted to.

The days in the hospital ran together after that. Feedings. Checks. Sleep that wasn't really sleep. The slow work of remembering my body was mine. I kept looking at her, trying to take her in, trying to feel what I had imagined I would feel. Some of it came. Some of it I am still waiting on.

We came home eventually. The story didn't end when we walked through the door. Some of it I am still inside.

I wish I could tell this in a more joyful way. Every time I come back to it, the sadness comes up first.

So many things went wrong. And I can't go back and fix them.

But she is here. And so am I.
